essence: David and Ian agreed the next brain/memory step is a retrieval benchmark harness (score quality, query/response time, write failure notes, find common failure modes, then a phased spec — no quick hacks), and David flagged that Martin's KBDE Kyber Extension SDK tightly couples extensions to the Electron chat app when a decoupled plugin/API with a stable backward-compatible contract would be more flexible.

actions.text: Build retrieval benchmark harness (quality/query-time/failure-note scoring) before writing the phased memory improvement spec
Pressure-test KBDE extension architecture toward decoupled plugin/API with backward-compatible contract

essence: David dictated the seed concept for a multi-stream conversational inbox: chain-of-consciousness streams (OMI first, later Krisp and YouTube, maybe email) flow into a central inbox that auto-tags, labels, routes, and summarizes via a tagging ontology — noting the existing Gemini tagging software is flaky; this is the Captain's Log precursor.

actions.text: Pull OMI transcripts from the API endpoint into a central inbox and run a tagging ontology over them

essence: David dictated full requirements for the YouTube Launch Optimizer app: ingest transcript + SRT (parsed to array), run the 12 v-appydave AWB YAML analysis prompts async without blocking the UX, generate title/thumbnail/thumbnail-text shortlists (10-20 → 6 → 3 finals, image-to-image text overlay on a bordered template, human-gated selection), build chapters from filming labels with a FliHub-style playback validator (jump 10s before, ±30s nudges), assemble description, and finish on a publish page with clipboard buttons and file locations for drag-into-YouTube.

actions.text: Draft detailed diagrammatic UX-first requirements spec (pages, decisions, data flow) for the YouTube Launch Optimizer

essence: David dictated requirements for an application management tool to track many micro apps (name, location, repo, groupings, tech-stack type: local AppyStack/socket.io, Next.js cloud, Ruby/JS CLI, always-on telemetry services) with reusable per-pattern requirements templates and structured JSON for website publishing, and spawned two sibling requirement sets: a skills management app and a YouTube video management app.

actions.text: Research the prior AppyDave 30-apps-in-30-days tracking experiment and draft app tracker requirements
Draft starter requirements for skills management and YouTube video management sibling apps, then decide where requirements docs live

essence: After failing to add an AWB alias, David discovered the jump skill had been modified to work around the jump CLI instead of using it (leaving the tool out of date), and decided appydave-tools needs a proper spec with full unit test coverage before the skill is refactored to use the standardized tooling — also directing Claude to be proactive with exact copy-pasteable commands, paths, and model/agent-team choices.

actions.text: Write a proper appydave-tools spec including unit tests and knowledge of all prior workaround changes
Refactor the jump skill to use the provided CLI tooling instead of working around it

essence: Mid-way through a 33-document planning set (the AWB d-prefixed docs), David asked how the code-focused product work would be reframed for marketing/selling the idea, and commissioned three marketing docs with an 'm' prefix plus completion of the outstanding d33 document.

actions.text: Write the d33 document to finish the d-series planning set
Write three m-prefixed marketing documents reframing the product for selling/explaining

essence: Note-to-self: get ghosty (tmux-based multi-agent swarm tool) working and prove it with two tests — a BMAD 6.7 workflow run to completion and a Haiku swarm (which may not need a full swarm) — while checking the brain for undocumented tmux/team-swarm issues and questioning whether AWB app documentation prep is still needed.

actions.text: Get ghosty working and run a BMAD 6.7 workflow to completion via a multi-agent swarm
Document outstanding tmux + multi-agent team swarm issues in the brain

essence: David downloaded a prerelease of Archon and used Claude Code to learn its model — YAML-defined workflows composed of node types (non-deterministic prompt nodes vs deterministic batch-command nodes) with list/execute commands — while figuring out how to make Archon work with BMAD.

David attended Cole Medin's four-hour Dynamous workshop on building a custom secure AI second brain — Claude Agent SDK + skills, Obsidian memory layer (soul/user/memory.md) with daily-log reflection/dream promotion, SQLite hybrid RAG, deterministic heartbeat cron, and lethal-trifecta zero-trust guardrails positioned against running OpenClaw — and asked how to share one brain across his 5-machine Tailscale fleet (answered via git vault-sync in the VPS deployment segment).

At a Chiang Mai AI hangout David and peers debated Claude subscription tiers vs Codex/Gemini and token economics (shared pool, prompt caching killed by mid-session model switches, offloading analysis to $20 GPT via his gpt-context concat tool), compared Cole Medin's revamped Archon YAML coding harness — 'a more primitive version of my Agent Workflow Builder' — with Paperclip's issue-to-agent orchestration, and swapped Anthropic culture stories (blameless first-day outage, Boris's public responses, /buddy Tamagotchi pets).
